skill.name: helm-chart-scaffolding
skill.description: Design and validate Helm chart scaffolds for packaging Kubernetes applications with reusable values, templates, and release metadata.
skill.summary: Scaffold production-oriented Helm charts with templates, values, dependencies, and validation guidance.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are false positives from Helm template syntax, placeholder URLs, and documentation examples. The main residual risk is the included validation script, which intentionally runs Helm and jq commands on a caller-provided chart directory and should be used only with trusted chart inputs.

medium_findings.title: Validation Script Executes Local Tooling on User-Supplied Chart Path
medium_findings.description: The validation script accepts a chart directory argument and runs Helm linting, template rendering, dry-run installation, dependency checks, and optional jq validation. This is expected for a Helm validation skill, but running it on untrusted charts or with a sensitive Kubernetes context can expose local chart contents, contact the configured cluster, or process malicious chart metadata through Helm.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly executes Helm and jq against a caller-selected path. The behavior is legitimate for validation, so the concern is operational exposure rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Example Commands Can Contact External Services When Copied
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes Helm repository and AWS upload examples. These are normal Helm distribution instructions, but users who copy them with real values may contact external repositories or upload chart packages.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are explicit network-capable commands, but they are documented examples rather than automatic behavior. No evidence shows hidden or unauthorized exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholder or Public Chart References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs flagged by static analysis are Helm metadata examples, placeholder project links, public Bitnami chart repository entries, localhost output, or Helm documentation links. They do not show hidden callbacks, tracking endpoints, or credential exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in chart metadata, examples, or reference links and match normal Helm documentation patterns. No evidence found of automated network calls to these URLs by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Template Delimiters Misclassified as Shell or Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command findings are caused by Helm Go-template delimiters and fenced documentation examples. The reviewed locations are instructional chart snippets rather than Ruby backticks or runtime shell execution by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is Helm templating and Markdown examples, not executable Ruby code. There is no evidence that these snippets run automatically during skill loading.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Secrets and Example Passwords Require Replacement
low_findings.description: The values template and examples include placeholder secret sections and the sample password changeme. This is not credential theft, but publishing real deployments from these defaults would be unsafe.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are visibly placeholders in example Helm configuration. They are weak defaults if copied unchanged, but no real secret or exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Reviewed skill instructions and reference files did not contain override language, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted searches and manual review found ordinary Helm guidance. This finding documents a negative result because prompt injection was specifically in scope.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Invokes Helm Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The script invokes helm lint, helm template, helm install --dry-run, helm dependency list, and jq against local files. These commands are expected but should be run only in controlled environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in the script. Their use is transparent and aligned with chart validation, so this is a controlled dangerous pattern rather than malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Includes Network-Capable CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The guidance includes helm repo and aws s3 examples that can contact external endpoints when a user intentionally runs them. They are not executed by the skill automatically.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples with clear DevOps purpose. The risk depends on user execution and supplied destinations.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes teams need repeatable Helm charts that are organized, configurable, and easy to validate. This skill guides chart structure, values design, templating patterns, dependencies, and release checks.
actual_capabilities: Explains standard Helm chart directory structure and required files.
Provides Chart.yaml and values.yaml templates for application charts.
Shows reusable Helm helper templates for names, labels, and image references.
Documents dependency, hook, test, and multi-environment values patterns.
Includes a shell validation script that runs Helm linting, rendering, and dry-run checks.
Highlights Kubernetes security defaults such as non-root pods and read-only root filesystems.
limitations: Does not generate a complete chart automatically without user-provided application details.
Validation requires local Helm, and optional schema checks require jq.
Examples use placeholder domains, repositories, image names, and passwords that users must replace.
Does not replace cluster-specific security review, policy checks, or production release approval.

use_cases.title: Create a New Application Chart
use_cases.description: Build a chart layout with metadata, values, templates, helpers, tests, and packaging guidance.

use_cases.title: Standardize Deployment Configuration
use_cases.description: Organize values for development, staging, and production while keeping templates reusable.

use_cases.title: Review Chart Readiness
use_cases.description: Use linting, template rendering, dry-run installation, and security checks before release.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me scaffold a Helm chart for a Kubernetes web application. Include the required files, values sections, and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Values for Environments
prompt_templates.prompt: Design values.yaml, values-staging.yaml, and values-prod.yaml for my service. Include image, replicas, resources, ingress, probes, and persistence.

prompt_templates.title: Add Dependencies and Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Extend my Helm chart with PostgreSQL, Redis, chart tests, and dependency conditions. Explain what each values section controls.

prompt_templates.title: Harden and Validate a Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Helm chart structure for production readiness. Focus on security context, resource limits, probes, network policy, hooks, and validation commands.

output_examples.input: Request to create a Helm chart for a web API with PostgreSQL and ingress.
output: A chart structure with Chart.yaml, values.yaml, templates, helpers, tests, and dependency sections.
Guidance for database dependency configuration, ingress hosts, probes, resources, and secure pod defaults.
A validation checklist covering linting, rendering, dry-run installation, dependencies, and schema checks.

output_examples.input: Request to prepare production values for an existing chart.
output: Recommended production settings for replicas, autoscaling, resource requests, resource limits, ingress, and persistence.
Notes about replacing placeholder secrets and avoiding hardcoded production credentials.
Commands to validate the rendered manifests before packaging or release.

output_examples.input: Request to review chart organization before publishing.
output: A review of required files, metadata, helper templates, dependency declarations, tests, and chart repository layout.
Warnings for missing values schema, missing Chart.lock, or incomplete security context settings.

best_practices: Replace all placeholder repositories, hosts, image names, maintainers, and example passwords before publishing a chart.
Run chart validation in a controlled workspace against trusted chart input and the intended Kubernetes context.
Keep values documented, dependency versions pinned, and security defaults enabled by default.
anti_patterns: Do not publish charts with placeholder domains, placeholder maintainers, or example credentials still present.
Do not run validation scripts against untrusted charts or sensitive kubeconfig contexts without review.
Do not store real secrets directly in values.yaml or chart templates.

faq.question: Does this skill create a full Helm chart automatically?
faq.answer: It provides structure, templates, examples, and validation guidance. The user still supplies application-specific details.

faq.question: Can it validate a chart locally?
faq.answer: Yes. The included script runs Helm linting, template rendering, dry-run installation, and basic manifest checks.

faq.question: Does the validation script modify the cluster?
faq.answer: The script uses Helm dry-run and template commands. Users should still run it only with trusted inputs and contexts.

faq.question: Are the example URLs production-ready?
faq.answer: No. They are placeholders or public documentation and chart repository examples that users must replace as needed.

faq.question: Does it include security recommendations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes non-root execution, read-only root filesystem, dropped capabilities, probes, resources, and network policy sections.

faq.question: Can this skill help with chart repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains packaging, repository indexes, dependency updates, and basic chart repository layout.
